text: Your attention works fine. You don't need to "fix" it. You just don't have a target that makes it easy to focus for months on end. You don't have a vision so strong that everything else becomes boring.
zh: 当愿景足够强大、让干扰显得无关紧要时，专注会自然变得容易。

text: Save any post or link, research what's trending, schedule content, and more: eden.so
zh: 保存任何帖子或链接、研究趋势、安排内容等：eden.so

text: If you don't know what to learn, start writing. Writing teaches you how to think, how to learn, and how to attract an audience that supports your work. Those 3 skills are needed more than ever.
zh: 不知道该学什么时，就去写作。它会同时训练思考、学习和建立受众的能力。

text: You can't focus because you never let yourself be bored. Actually bored. Not "bored" scrolling on your phone taxing your brain into oblivion with more dopamine in 10 minutes than people a thousand years ago would get in an entire month.
zh: 持续刺激消灭了真正的无聊，而没有真正的无聊，大脑就很难专注。

text: Skills that have nothing to do with money but are worth dedicating an immense amount of practice to: - Charisma - Metacognition - Critical thinking - Sitting with discomfort - Articulating what you believe and why - Changing your beliefs when presented with new information Almost nobody actually practices these and it shows.
zh: 刻意练习魅力、元认知、批判思维、承受不适、清晰表达信念，以及改变看法的能力。

text: If you don't wake up excited and go to bed tired, drop everything and think of what your future will look like if you keep repeating the same day for the rest of your life. Sit with that discomfort until a new direction appears.
zh: 如果每天都一模一样，就与这种不适感待在一起，直到更有意义的方向浮现。

text: The fastest way to change your life is to rip yourself out of your (physical and digital) environment. Change everything overnight. The places you go, the accounts you follow, the info you consume, etc. It's difficult but it absolutely works.
zh: 改变物理环境和数字环境，往往比只依赖意志力更快地重置行为。

text: Stress is great for brute-forcing mindless tasks, but it absolutely destroys your ability to think spatially. It destroys your creativity, which is the one thing that will give you a competitive advantage over everyone optimizing for productivity.
zh: 压力或许能推动简单任务，却会收窄思维，损害真正形成优势的创造力。

text: You only need 1 hour. 1 hour of building. 1 hour of writing. 1 hour of lifting. 1 hour of studying. 1 hour of any form of bettering yourself, because it quickly compounds. 1 hour feels like nothing until you look back 365 hours later and everything's changed.
zh: 每天专注一小时去创造、写作、训练或学习，长期复利会塑造完全不同的人生。

text: The most important decision of your life
zh: 您一生中最重要的决定

text: If you don't have a goal so meaningful it makes other people's opinions irrelevant, you will lose control of your life. You will adopt the goals assigned to you by your parents, peers, or society.
zh: 如果您没有一个有意义的目标，以至于其他人的意见无关紧要，您将失去对生活的控制。您将采用...

text: The 2-4 hours you spend scrolling each day (or 730-1460 hours each year) is more than enough time to write a book, build a business, or get in shape. In the moment, it seems like nothing. That's why it's so dangerous. Your time disappears without you being conscious of it.
zh: 每天刷手机流失的时间，足够写一本书、建立一项事业，或彻底改变身体。

text: Yes, anyone can build anything now. We know. But it wasn't that hard to do before. You execute a series of steps toward a goal. The problem is psychological. Most people will still fail to start, and even more will fail to iterate. Your competition isn't as large as you think.
zh: 是的，现在任何人都可以构建任何东西。我们知道。但以前做起来并不难。你执行一系列步骤来实现...

text: How to master any skill fast: - stop studying - outline a project - start building it - hit a roadblock - figure out how to overcome it - repeat 4 and 5 for the rest of your life Most people don't get past 1, the rest spiral into complacency after 4.
zh: 如何快速掌握任何技能： - 停止学习 - 概述一个项目 - 开始构建它 - 遇到障碍 - 弄清楚...

text: Normalize disappearing for an extended period of time to do a factory reset on your focus and creativity.
zh: 有意识地消失一段时间，可以像恢复出厂设置一样重启专注力与创造力。

text: Read more here: letters.thedankoe.com/p/life-is-a-mi…
zh: 阅读更多内容：letters.thedankoe.com/p/life-is-a-mi...

text: Most people quit because they forget that you have to be bad at something before you can be good at it. It's so obvious. You suck. Of course you're not going to win in 2 weeks. But if you can learn to enjoy extended periods of failure, you will make it very, very far in life.
zh: 大多数人退出是因为他们忘记了你必须先不擅长某件事才能擅长它。这太明显了……

text: You aren't going to get lucky repeating the same day. You need to go on more side quests. Weekly. Daily if you can. Even one tiny new decision can substantially increase your chances at luck.
zh: 你不会幸运地重复同一天。你需要去完成更多的支线任务。每周。如果可以的话，每天都可以。甚至……

text: If you feel lost, build something. A business. Your body. A skill set. Anything that gives you a reason to learn and focus. Don't worry about choosing the right thing. Don't think about how difficult it will be. Just start moving forward and you'll find a path that feels right
zh: 感到迷失时，就创造某样东西，让自己有理由学习、专注并继续前进。

text: The greatest trait you can acquire is to work with tremendous intensity on things that matter to you, and more importantly, be strangely unbothered when those things don't work out.
zh: 你能获得的最大特质就是以极大的强度去做对你来说重要的事情，更重要的是，奇怪地不受打扰……

text: If you want a rare life, you have to be delusional. Doubt can enter your mind, and it can sound reasonable, but if you entertain it too much it will slowly drag you down into stagnation. I'd rather reap the lesson from massive failure than do nothing because it's not "realistic."
zh: 不寻常的人生，需要容纳看似不理性的信念、怀疑，以及巨大失败带来的教训。

text: At some point, usually in your 20s, you'll notice that the people around you stop believing in themselves. And no matter how hard you try, you can't save them. By all means, do not let it infect your mind. Stay on your path.
zh: 别人失去信念也会传染。与其拯救所有人，不如先保护好自己的方向。

text: You need to read more, and you need to consume less. You need to set aside time to do a workout for the mind, and you need to give it time to recover. Especially now that it's so easy for your cognitive capacity to atrophy. Refuse to become mentally obese.
zh: 你需要阅读更多，你需要消耗更少。你需要留出时间锻炼一下头脑，...

text: If you want to achieve anything great, it needs to become your one true priority. The only thing on your mind. Nobody accidentally got rich from business. Nobody accidentally built a great physique. They were obsessed with it for multiple years until it became their default.
zh: 伟大的结果很少偶然发生。在一段时期内，目标必须成为真正的优先事项。

text: A pattern I've noticed in stuck people: They're always busy. They never stop moving. They have 47 tabs open and a notebook-sized to-do list. But if you ask them what they accomplished this week that actually matters, their mind goes blank. Busyness isn't a badge of honor.
zh: 持续忙碌可能只是在掩盖一个事实：整周都没有取得真正重要的进展。

text: If you're a writer, be okay with repeating what's already been said, because most ideas have already been exhausted, and genuinely novel ideas are rare. Nobody follows you because you're talking about something new. They follow you because they want to read your point of view.
zh: 如果你是一名作家，重复已经说过的话就可以了，因为大多数想法已经用尽，真正新颖的想法是...

text: You as a single person have more power today than a 20 person company of the past. That's insane. The internet gave you the ability to learn anything. Social media gave you the leverage to reach anyone. AI is giving you the ability to create almost anything. Please don't waste it
zh: 今天你作为一个人比过去 20 人的公司更有力量。那太疯狂了。互联网给了你…

text: Write more essays: letters.thedankoe.com/p/im-begging-y…
zh: 写更多文章：letters.thedankoe.com/p/im-begging-y…

text: You need to write more. Without AI. Without templates. Without knowing what you're writing about. Just you, an idea, and enough time to do the difficult cognitive work necessary to reach true understanding. If you don't, your ability to think will drastically decline.
zh: 你需要写更多。没有人工智能。没有模板。不知道你在写什么。只有你、一个想法和足够的时间去做……

text: The more "informed" you feel, the less capable of thinking you become.
zh: 你感觉自己越“见多识广”，你的思考能力就越弱。

text: You can learn anything in 2 weeks. You can't master it, obviously, but if you obsess over it, you can become better at it than most people ever will. You'd be surprised how fast your life can change when you understand this.
zh: 两周高强度、窄范围的练习不会带来精通，却足以形成令人惊讶的能力。

text: It seems like everyone is obsessed with productivity and efficiency yet rarely get anything meaningful done. I'm convinced your best work is done when you're not working. When you have space for creative ideas to emerge that drastically change the trajectory of your life/work.
zh: 似乎每个人都痴迷于生产力和效率，但却很少完成任何有意义的事情。我相信你最好的工作是在以下情况下完成的：

text: Competition is largely an illusion. 95% of people don't even try to do great things. 0.1% of the people are loud, so you overestimate how many people there are. The rest get stuck worrying about competition and quitting after 2 weeks.
zh: 竞争看起来比实际更大，因为多数人从未开始，或者几周后就放弃了。

text: Habits so simple you think they’re not worth doing, but have a profound impact on your life: - Not touching your phone when you wake up - Not thinking about work after work is done - Putting a book down once you find an idea worth thinking about - Setting aside time to do nothing for 10 minutes a day - Going on a short walk after each meal - Eating a meal without a screen in front of you - Saying "I don't know" instead of pretending you do - Asking "What if this isn't actually a problem?" before trying to solve it - Letting yourself be bad at something instead of expecting perfection - Trying to understand something you disagree with instead of looking for flaws - Defaulting to "no" until you think through the commitment
zh: 习惯如此简单，你认为它们不值得做，但却对你的生活产生深远的影响： - 当你......

text: "I'm not a creative person." No, you are, everyone is, but your mind is just clogged by all of the podcasts and social media you ram into it without properly digesting it. You're conditioned to believe you can only take a certain path in life, and you don't daydream or entertain stupid ideas that could set you on an entirely new trajectory. You're so obsessed with being productive and efficient that you feel like you're always falling behind, and that stress prevents you from thinking outside the box. You need to slow the fuck down, allow yourself to be bored (actually bored, not so overstimulated that you find enjoyable things boring), and pursue a life that you design, not one that was assigned to you.
zh: “我不是一个有创造力的人。”不，你是，每个人都是，但你的思想只是被所有播客和社交媒体堵塞了......

text: Your brain works against you until you give it a meaningful goal to wire itself around.
zh: 在找到一个值得围绕它组织自身的目标之前，大脑会一直抵抗分散的努力。

text: Copy-pasting into one chat box sucks. So we built a canvas for LLMs, content, and research. Create markdown docs, paste social links, upload files, and connect to AI chats. Agents can handle busy work, but creative work needs a visual space to connect ideas.
zh: 复制粘贴到一个聊天框中很糟糕。因此，我们为法学硕士、内容和研究构建了一个画布。创建 Markdown 文档、粘贴社交链接、上传文件……

text: The single most important thing you can do in today's world is to stop operating from the old paradigm. If you need to be told what to do next (go to school, get a job, retire at 65) the outcome of your life will always be in someone else's hands. You must learn how to direct your own work. You must learn how to tolerate and mitigate risk and uncertainty. You must figure out what you want and teach yourself everything necessary to get it. It's extremely difficult, but not as difficult as the silent suffering people learn to accept as "normal."
zh: 在当今世界，您可以做的最重要的一件事就是停止按照旧的范例进行操作。如果您需要……

text: It doesn't matter if it takes years. What the fuck else are you going to do? You aren't going to learn, create, experiment, grow, and everything that makes life enjoyable because you have something better to be doing? Your mind is playing tricks on you.
zh: 即使需要数年时间也没关系。你他妈的还要做什么？你不会学习、创造、实验、成长……

text: The minute I become too frugal or money-conscious is the moment it starts to control me and becomes difficult to make. When I spend almost carelessly, it seems to find its way back. Money-induced stress narrows the mind and new opportunities become invisible.
zh: 当我变得过于节俭或注重金钱的那一刻，它就开始控制我并且变得很难做。当我花费...

text: The problem is that you don't know what you want to do, and figuring out what you want to do requires learning, experimentation, and effort - so you do nothing.
zh: 问题是你不知道自己想做什么，而弄清楚自己想做什么需要学习、实验和...

text: Polymaths will dominate the next 5 years, but only if they practice the skill of knowing what to ignore. You can learn and do anything now, meaning that it will become increasingly rare for a person to put time, attention, and care into one thing.
zh: 博学者将在未来5年占据主导地位，但前提是他们练习知道要忽略什么的技能。你可以学习并做...

text: The secret is to have something you're excited to do when you wake up. It takes a few months of learning, failure, and experimentation to find what that thing is, but once you do, everything is just... better.
zh: 秘诀是当你醒来时做一些你兴奋的事情。这需要几个月的学习、失败和实验……

text: Focus is becoming the rarest skill on the planet. Talent, intelligence, marketing, prompting, vibe coding, and whatever else doesn't matter as much as being able to channel your attention for an extended period of time into one useful thing that you want to see in the world.
zh: 专注正在成为地球上最稀有的技能。天赋、智力、营销、提示、氛围编码以及其他任何东西都不重要，重要的是……

text: Self-improvement is a gateway drug into entrepreneurship because you start seeing life as a system you can optimize. You start to believe that if you don't like something about your life, you can change it. And since the source of most people's problems is money, you stop waiting for a raise and start building something.
zh: 自我完善是进入创业的入门药物，因为你开始将生活视为一个可以优化的系统。你开始相信，如果……

text: The ultimate goal is getting paid to be yourself. To solve problems you can't help but solve. To study what you can't pull yourself away from. To eliminate any work that does not challenge you creatively.
zh: 最终目标是获得报酬，做你自己。为了解决你无法不去解决的问题。去研究那些你自己无法拉动的东西……

text: The easiest way to get ahead is to commit to a period of skill development. 6-12 months. Pure focus for 2-4 hours a day. Learning and building. Not just binge watching tutorials, but creating quality projects that you, others, or businesses could actually benefit from. But don't just let those projects sit around. Tell the world about them. See if people care enough to pay you. Fix what doesn't work until they do. That's it. That's what most people need to completely turn their life around.
zh: 用六到十二个月专注学习、做真实项目、接受公开反馈并迭代，足以改变职业轨迹。

text: Grab a notebook. Write out exactly what you want in your future, don't miss a detail. Then, break it down into goals. Decades, years, months, weeks, and days. Every day, write down the 3 levers you can pull to make actual progress. Then, forget about the goals and execute.
zh: 详细描述未来，把它拆成不同时间尺度，找出今天能撬动的杠杆，然后执行。

text: I'll be creating a part 2 of this next week, but here's a good place to start: letters.thedankoe.com/p/how-to-think…
zh: 我将在下周创建第二部分，但这里是一个很好的起点：letters.thedankoe.com/p/how-to-think...

text: The best thing you can do in today's world is practice thinking. Very few people, especially smart people, do this. Snap yourself out of your automatic patterns and stretch your mind for once. This will only become more important as AI and social media distort reality.
zh: 在当今世界，你能做的最好的事情就是练习思考。很少有人，尤其是聪明人，会这样做。让自己摆脱……

text: It'll be interesting to watch how it plays out that's for sure. I don't think there will "always be room" by any means, but I've seen quite a few people do well with tiny audiences. A tight knit little tribe can go a long way for those who think their only chances come from competing with the big dogs
zh: 观看它的表现肯定会很有趣。我不认为无论如何都会有“永远有空间”，但是…

text: I do think there can be a day that is an inflection point in your life that you think back on often as "the moment my life changed" - so yes, but I also attempt to make it very clear that many people may not reach that point just by reading the article. Much of the point of the article is that it can't always be forced That's the beauty of long form, its much more than a tweet or headline
zh: 我确实认为有一天是你生命中的转折点，你经常回想起“…

text: In all seriousness it is a good take. That’s what some people need. But there’s also a reason why “just do the work” hasn’t solved all the world’s problems. My articles obviously don’t either, but I don’t think it’s a crime for me to find it interesting enough to write about and others to find it interesting enough to spend 20-30 min reading Elonbucks are nice too As is Coelho
zh: 严肃地说，这是一个很好的选择。这就是有些人需要的。但是还有一个原因为什么“只做工作”没有…

text: There are incredible new books, but I feel like books have become similar to social media. Authors are writing to hit NYT rather than for the love of the game - which is fine, I don't blame them at all - but when you write from that frame you make decisions to water things down, make them more attention grabbing for the sake of it, and strip the timelessness from it. Most (not all) are writing for a quick hit and a revenue spike so they can close speaking gigs, get on podcasts, etc. The book is more of a lever for more influence rather than something they think the world would benefit from.
zh: 有令人难以置信的新书，但是我觉得书籍已经变得类似于社交媒体，而不是......

text: The good thing about X is that people are quick to call things out, much tighter feedback loop than other platforms I trust they'll be able to figure it out, but i also do think there will always be a market for that kind of content. Some people are very good at hijacking attention (not a bad skill to have) but I agree the value should be there to match
zh: X 的好处是人们能很快地指出事情，比我相信他们会的其他平台更严格的反馈循环......

text: $1 million dollar prizes aside, I think there's been a quiet hunger for articles. The pendulum swung too far in the direction of TikTok and endless scrolling and the internet has a chance to self-correct. I don't think the human brain was meant to be ripped in a thousand different directions as soon as you get on your phone. Too much chaos that has downstream affects on the quality of everyday life. Long form content allows you to focus on set of ideas. It brings order to the mind. You learn more, you feel better, and usually you're left with something you can actually apply to your life, even if it's just a better way of thinking. Especially with AI, we've gotten to the point where we think we want things as short as possible. We have this deranged desire to "get to the point" when the point is to sit with it. You can collect all of the tips and tricks you want but you're completely missing the context that allows you to turn those tips into results. A bullet list of actionable steps will do absolutely nothing for you. Pre-2015 books are the best option, but articles are a great step in what I think is the right direction.
zh: 除了 100 万美元的奖金之外，我认为人们对文章的渴望偏向了 TikTok 的方向......

text: The primary reason people don't achieve big goals is because they don't realize that the only source of truth is making mistakes. They soak up advice and theory, expecting it to be an exact match to their mind, experience, and situation just to fail once and give up completely.
zh: 主要原因人们无法实现大目标是因为他们没有意识到真理的唯一来源是犯错误。他们会浸泡...

text: You don't magically become focused. You practice it. You notice you're distracted and snap out of it, over and over again until it's second nature. Most people have not trained this muscle, and they are not anywhere close to as focused as they think they are.
zh: 你不会神奇地变得专注。你会发现自己分心了，然后一次又一次地摆脱它，直到第二次...

text: be harder on yourself
zh: 对自己更严格

text: Your life is determined by the amount of uncertainty you're willing to embrace.
zh: 你的生活取决于你愿意接受的不确定性。

text: If you don't shamelessly promote your work, every day for the next 2-3 years, you will get stuck doing someone else's work until you realize that nobody is going to pay you if they have no idea who you are.
zh: 如果你不无耻地推广你的工作，在接下来的 2-3 年里，你每天都会被困在别人的工作中，直到你……

text: If you have multiple interests, don't let anyone convince you that you should narrow your focus. You may be confused for a while, but if you stick it out, you will blow past everyone else.
zh: 如果你有多种兴趣，不要让任何人说服你应该缩小你的焦点，你可能会感到困惑一段时间，但是……

text: The deeper you fall into a rut the more fuel you have to launch out of it, but also the more excuses you have to stay the same. It is very important that you see through your own bullshit and choose the first option.
zh: 你陷入困境越深，你需要摆脱困境的燃料就越多，但你的借口也就越多……

text: The greatest focus hack is to know what you're working on, why you're working on it, when it needs to be done, and how exactly to get it done.
zh: 最大的焦点技巧是知道你正在做什么，为什么要做它，什么时候需要完成，以及如何......

text: If you work so that you never have to work again, you'll be disappointed when retirement leaves you feeling empty. Find work that you would do after retirement as early as you can and you'll never feel the need to retire.
zh: 如果你工作是为了永远不必再工作，那么当退休让你感到空虚时，你会感到失望。找到你...的工作

text: Don't try to learn the "best new skills." Build your own thing. Try to sell it. Fail over and over again. Learn what it means to adapt and iterate. Remove your dependency from others and hunt for your own survival. You will learn the exact skills you need, and you will learn them better than anyone else.
zh: 不要试图学习“最好的新技能”。建立你自己的东西。尝试卖掉它。一次又一次的失败。了解它是什么...

text: As someone who was against AI for a long time, AI is a skill just like any other, and the usefulness of AI increases as you get better at it. The first time you play a guitar, you literally can't do anything. You sound like shit. But you slowly get better. You can play more songs. You enjoy it so much more. We were made to think that AI would just solve all our problems and do everything for us, so people were disappointed when it didn't do those things, but if you actually try to learn it, then you will see how helpful it can be. That sounds like a cop out, but it's silly to think that AI is just going to solve all your problems out of the box. If you don't use it for an extended period of time then (as with every other skill) you will not be able to get the best results with it. It won't remove all of your work, or any of it for that matter if you don't want it to (I've been spending more time working, because it's fun), but it will give you a deeper, more enjoyable way of doing what you already do. You abstract out a layer from physical labor to mental labor. You spend more time in the realm of ideas and taste. It starts to feel more like a game or puzzle than it does "work." You have to be okay with moving from doing the manual grunt work of writing lines of code or prose to discerning whether or not it sounds like you want it to sound. I don't think anyone, aside from other writers who haven't detached from the labor, would argue that hiring a ghostwriter (i.e. James Patterson) is cheating. The end readers still love it. He is still in control of the plot, vision, and what actually gets published. Nobody would argue that the director of a film doesn't deserve praise because he is not manually filming it or building the set. There is going to be, and there already is, so much pushback on AI for valid reasons, but most of those reasons are psychological defense mechanisms tying you to how you've identified yourself with how you work. AI doesn't have to be a religion. You don't have to use it for everything. But I don't think it's a wise idea to willingly choose a side that is going to lose.
zh: 作为长期反对人工智能的人，人工智能就像其他任何技能一样，是一种技能，而且人工智能的有用性不断增加……

text: If you want to avoid being replaced, stop worrying about which skills will be relevant 20 years from now. Nobody has a single clue what those are going to be. The world is changing so rapidly that you can’t even imagine what skills will be valuable in the future. Instead, you need to learn how to learn fast, because if you can become effective at any skill quicker than other people, it doesn’t matter what skill you learn. But that isn’t something you just sit down and study. You must step into the arena. You must learn by doing. You must build your own thing. Your own project. Your own product. Then you must share it with others to determine whether it’s valuable or not. Why? Because that forces you to learn efficiently. Everything you learn is directly applied to a meaningful project that allows you to not be dependent on an employer that requires you to learn a single skill that they will replace as soon as they can.
zh: 如果你想避免被取代，就不要再担心哪些技能在 20 年后仍然适用。没有人有任何线索……

text: You don't achieve the life you want then become the "successful" person. You become the person first, which looks cringe until it becomes normal, and success eventually catches up to you. You choose the character you want to play, and only then do the right quests appear.
zh: 你没有实现你想要的生活，然后成为“成功”的人。你首先成为那个看起来令人畏缩的人，直到它变得正常，......

text: You easily quit your goals because there's nothing on the line. You don't have a vision for a better life that is so clear that your current life feels like losing if you don't reach it. Secretly most people don't actually want to achieve their goals. They want to impress others and get a dopamine hit from telling them their goals.
zh: 你很容易放弃你的目标，因为没有什么可以赌的。你没有如此清晰的美好生活愿景……

zh: 关于如何改变的 7 个更多想法和协议：letters.thedankoe.com/p/how-to-fix-y...

text: The fastest way to change is to obsessively reflect on what you do every day and do not lie to yourself about what kind of life that is creating.

text: Your life flips upside down when you realize that "who you are" is a program written by people who were also programmed, and unless you make the painful choice to rewrite that code, most of your thoughts and actions are not beneficial to your future, because they are not yours.
zh: 当你意识到“你是谁”是一个由同样被编程的人编写的程序时，你的生活就会发生翻天覆地的变化，除非……

text: You aren't where you want to be because you don't have the mind of the person who would be in that position. You're exactly where you should be for your level of development, and changing that is within your control. This goes deeper than you think: youtu.be/K8K09g9XR4s
zh: 你没有达到你想要的位置，因为你没有处于那个位置的人的思想。你正是……

text: We're going through one of those historical shifts where there are 3 types of people: 1) People who cry and become violent about the fact that they can't "do what they've been doing" for the rest of their lives without getting left behind (avoid these people) 2) People who do nothing until they are forced to because they have to survive, but the penalty for starting late is so severe because everyone else is exponentially further ahead of them 3) People who stay curious, experiment, build, and figure out how to adopt the new way of doing things in a way thats useful (without attaching to the way things were done in the past) It has always been important that you choose option 3, but now is as good a time as ever.
zh: 我们正在经历历史性的转变之一，其中有 3 种类型的人：1）那些因……而哭泣并变得暴力的人。

text: Do everything in your power to never become helpless. It's a dangerous and lonely hole. Helpful people will stay as far away from you as they can because they know that you will not do anything with their help. Do not complain without being open to a solution. Do not complain if you know that you are going to do absolutely nothing to change. Do not expect anyone to give you anything. Nobody cares how bad your situation is if you clearly show that you do not want to escape it.
zh: 尽你所能，永远不要变得无助。这是一个危险而孤独的洞。乐于助人的人会离你越远...

text: You aren't where you want to be because you don't want to be there more than you want to be where you are. Your behavior is your confession. You say you want a better life (mostly for social approval), but when something more instant and pleasurable comes around, your actions show that you want that more.
zh: 你没有到达你想去的地方，因为你不想去那里，而不是你想去那里。你的…

text: The future belongs to the high agency. Sounds cute. Everyone thinks they're high agency. But most people aren't. About 50% of the population do not have the cognitive development to be so. Here's why it's more important than ever and how to practice it: youtu.be/0XI_Xt0ci2Y

text: Your best ideas will never come from trying to have them. You must let the creative process play out automatically. You must read more, do more, experience more. Then you must give yourself time to do nothing because that's when your mind will put all the pieces together and shoot out an idea that may change everything.
zh: 你最好的想法永远不会来自尝试拥有它们。你必须让创作过程自动进行。你一定要多读，...

text: When everyone has an advantage, it is no longer an advantage. text: Signs you're on the right track: Work energizes you Learning doesn't take effort Discipline doesn't need to be forced You think in decades, not days You're excited to wake up You relate to very few people, but the relationships you do form are irreplaceable.
zh: 标志您走在正确的轨道上：工作让您充满活力学习不需要努力纪律不需要强迫您认为几十年后，不会...

text: If you can't articulate yourself, it will be difficult to get what you want in life. Customers won't care about your product. Employers won't give you a raise. Readers won't give you their attention. Try this: youtu.be/DKT6m_8vCkA

text: When your days become too mundane, you need to change something fast. You need chaos. You need novelty. You need the experiences and ideas that inspire you to go on your next quest. Build something new. Rip apart your daily routine. Do anything but the same thing.
zh: 当你的日子变得太平凡时，你需要快速改变一些事情。你需要混乱。你需要新奇。你需要经验和想法...

text: If you aren't building something that leads to the life you want, even if it's "only" 30-60 minutes a day, you will by default stay in the same life you're in, building things that allow others to live the life they want. 30 minutes can, in fact, start the snowball.
zh: 如果你没有建造一些东西来实现你想要的生活，即使每天“仅”30-60 分钟，默认情况下你也会...

text: If you're bored, create something. Anything. Software. Designs. Photos. Videos. Writing. A business. A body. A relationship. A mind. How can you not be excited to wake up when you have the ability to create.
zh: 如果你感到无聊，那就创造一些东西。任何事物。软件。设计。照片。视频。写作。一项业务。一个身体。一种关系。一个头脑。你怎么能不……

text: You would learn faster if you actually started building the thing.
zh: 如果你真正开始构建这个东西，你会学得更快。

text: You can train your mind to achieve anything you want. zh: 你可以训练你的思维来实现你想要的任何事情。大多数你所没有的只不过是缺乏技能，...

zh: 微妙提醒您应该感到不知所措。这意味着你正在做一些新的事情。你正在走一条新路。你正在学习。你正在成长。你...

text: Self-discipline isn't difficult, you're just distracted. The mind craves order. That’s why you feel out of control. Your attention is not focused on a singular vision for the future so potent that distractions can’t penetrate it. Feel deep into your situation. Realize where your life will end up if you keep going down this path. Become absolutely fed up with the lack of progress you are making. Use that negative energy to slingshot toward a meaningful goal you’ve been putting off. I know you can hear that voice that keeps nagging at you saying, you were meant for more than this. Discipline comes from clarity, not force. Discipline is a feature of identity. You aren’t disciplined because you aren’t the type of person who seamlessly does what they need to do. It isn’t difficult for a writer to sit down and write. It isn’t difficult for a bodybuilder to eat healthy. In fact, it’s difficult for them not to eat healthy. They hate it. They do it because that’s who they are. They don’t have options. They deeply understand the impact of being distracted from their goal. Success is automatic. If you want to be disciplined, stop trying to be. Instead, remove every single distraction that prevents you from being disciplined. Give yourself one option. Shut your mind off and execute.
zh: 自律并不难，只是你分心了。头脑渴望秩序。这就是为什么你感觉失控。你的注意力没有集中在……

text: You can't improve what doesn't exist. The ideas in your head. The business you dream of building. The plans you keep putting off. All are worthless until you test them against reality. You're afraid of failure and glorify perfection when the opposite is what determines success.

zh: 为什么每周工作 40 小时是个笑话：youtu.be/mA0P-5_QvH8

zh: 如果你没有重要的事情需要关心，你自然会关心那些让你愚蠢、愤怒和沮丧的事情。

text: Most people's lives are determined by how they choose to cure their boredom.

zh: 如果你明白你会成为你所读、所看、所听的人，或你周围的人，那么你就不会做90%的事…

text: It's pretty simple. Wake up early. Complete the single highest priority task. Avoid the destination society set for you at birth. Make giant mistakes. Mistakes are the launchpads toward your ideal life. Always be building a project. Youth is the greatest currency, it would be wise to invest it. If you wasted your youth, crying about it isn't going to speed up the process. You're supposed to feel overwhelmed, it means you're learning. Set aside 30 minutes daily for deliberate boredom (to unfuck your dopamine sensitivity). Walk more. Read more. Write more. Writing is how you practice thinking. Thinking is how you make better decisions. The outcome of your life is determined by the sum of your decisions. Force yourself to do something you've never done before, at least once a week. Learn something new. Talk to someone new. Book a one way plane ticket. Move into that place you can't afford. Because you need pressure. Real pressure. Launch yourself into the situation of your future self and see if you have what it takes.
zh: 这很简单。早点起床。完成单个最高优先级任务。避开社会在你出生时为你设定的目的地。犯巨大的错误。...

text: The glaring problem with schools is that a topic could take 1 week to learn, but it’s stretched across 3-4 months because that’s how long the course must be. Even worse, the learning doesn’t involve doing, so most people don’t learn a thing until they graduate.

text: The best way to stand out as a creator, in my eyes, is to take a weird topic you're passionate about and make it palatable to a larger audience. Don't follow the trends, but take the principles of human nature that make the trends work and apply them to your personal interests.

text: I don’t know of a single successful person who doesn’t have the skill of figuring it out. They don’t need to ask how to do something, because they deeply understand that they will have to fail, change direction, fail again, and repeat that process even if someone gave them the exact roadmap.
zh: 我不知道有哪个成功人士不具备解决这个问题的能力。他们不需要问如何...

text: The worst life is to wake up and have nothing you want to do. The best life is not being able to stop yourself from launching out of bed, working on a project that's important to you, and having so much clarity that stress and worry can't even find their way into your brain.
zh: 最糟糕的生活就是醒来后却无所事事。最好的生活就是无法阻止自己......

text: Learn how to do it yourself before you let AI do it for you. If you don't, you will not understand why it was done that way, why it got results, or why it didn't. You will become more and more dependent on a tool rather than your own creative ability.

text: Rare traits I’ve noticed in people who go on to do great things: 1. They were expected by their parents to enter a specific career, and it felt extremely limiting 2. They could clearly see that others achieved great things even when the odds were stacked against them, so they rightfully assumed they could too 3. They naturally questioned what was possible, and when teachers, family, or friends couldn’t give them good answers, they took it upon themselves to find them
zh: 我注意到那些继续做伟大事情的人具有罕见的特质： 1. 他们的父母期望他们进入一个特定的……

text: I love entrepreneurship because it allows you to apply anything you learn. At school, you’re told what to learn. At a job, you’re told what to work on, and you only learn until you are good enough to do the job (unless you self-educate out of genuine interest, but being honest, an abysmally low amount of people do that, because those who do realize that in order to learn they must grow, and you can’t grow in someone else’s box). When you do your own thing, the only way to survive is to do something you’ve never done before, which requires you to learn what can’t be taught in school.
zh: 我喜欢创业，因为它可以让你应用你所学到的任何东西。在学校，你被告知要学什么。在工作中，你被告知……

text: A pattern I’ve noticed in successful people - that didn’t sacrifice their life for success - is that they physically worked very little, yet people see them as hard workers. Mentally, they were always thinking, plotting, and scheming. They worked in their mind. And once they were clear on their idea, they executed with speed that others couldn’t compete with.
zh: 有些成功者表面工作时间更少，因为他们深度思考，并在清晰后迅速执行。

text: The best way to learn something is to write about it, in public, but only if you care about learning. When you write, you are forced to organize thoughts into words. When you teach someone else, you feel obligated to understand the topic.

text: If you want to do something that has never been done before, don't choose a career. Choose a great work. A mission. Something that you can dedicate decades of your life to. People won't believe in you, and it may feel lonely at times, but for some, it is the only life worth living.

text: Sometimes, you need to become so disgusted with yourself, your life, or your situation that you can’t help but change. The work you didn’t care about doing in the first place. The relationships that have grown to be repetitive and stale. The body that doesn’t feel electric anymore. There’s a big difference between being hard on yourself and being so easy on yourself that it becomes the hardest thing in the world. Nobody is going to do it for you. In fact, most people don’t want you to change. Don’t focus on the positive. Focus on the negative, then ruthlessly redirect that energy toward a meaningful future.

zh: 写了一封多巴胺排毒信：letters.thedankoe.com/p/a-dopamine-d...

text: Your brain thinks normal life is boring because it's gotten used to constant euphoria. You see more on your phone in 30 minutes than people used to in their entire lives. You eat foods that are so sweet that, once you stop, they become disgusting. If you dropped any and all sources of instant pleasure for the next 30 days, you'd feel like a child again. Life would feel electric.
zh: 你的大脑认为正常的生活很无聊，因为它已经习惯了持续的欣快感。 30 分钟内，您在手机上看到的内容比…

text: You don't have ideas because you don't read. You can't articulate ideas because you don't write. You can't leverage ideas because you don't build. You feel bored, anxious, or overwhelmed because your mind wants ideas to flow but you won't let them.

text: It's liberating once you realize that the mind can be trained to do anything you want. That most of what you lack can be changed with knowledge, skill, and a logical sequence of steps that victims will attempt to convince you don't exist.

text: You're allowed to be motivated by money. Scratch that, you are motivated by money. But you hide it because your parents, teachers, and peers convinced you it was a bad thing to pursue. You work, right now, for money, to survive. Don't apologize for wanting to live well.

text: A bit of info about us: Our mission is to pioneer the future of creative work and create a tool that allows people to be more creative with AI, not less. Previously we built Kortex, but due to a few problems with how we built it (full story for later, Kortex users have already received multiple updates about this), we decided to rebuild the app from scratch. Luckily, our plan has gone according to plan, and over the past few months we've built something that blows the last version of Kortex out of the water. We have the base of the mobile app built, but need another dev so we can stop splitting focus. If you'd like to work with a great team and myself in making Eden a household name for creatives, apply above.

text: I get unnecessarily mad when people don't stick to their goals. You say you want to start a business, lose weight, meet new people, whatever. You say you want to change your life because you're tired of where you are. Someone tells you exactly what to do, gives you the entire roadmap. Then you don't do it. And you can't give any good reason why. "Oh I have to work and got sidetracked." "I didn't go to the gym because I had something else do to." What the fuck are you talking about? You say you "have" to work and "have" to do whatever else you're doing in the life you just said you don't want to live, but you don't HAVE to do the only things that lead to the life you want? Everyone is obsessed with getting "actionable advice" when that clearly doesn't matter at all. Your psychology is in the toilet. Your priorities are nonexistent. You need to take a day off and really think about what you are doing. "Oh Dan be more empathetic!" That IS empathy. Empathy is wanting the best for someone even if it hurts their little feelings. Most people will simply pander to you so that they feel good about not making you feel bad. That's nowhere close to empathy. Do better.
zh: 当人们不坚持他们的目标时，我会不必要地生气。你说你想创业，减肥，结识新朋友，...

text: Just copy what works and make it slightly better. If you're starting out, it is so incredibly unlikely that you have the experience to do something that's 100% new. text: If you're trying to get exposure on your work, it sounds weird, but starting a podcast is one of the best things you can do. Not because you want to make it the number 1 podcast in the world, but because: 1) High status people / people you respect would rather be on a podcast than on a call with you. They're more likely to respond and accept, meaning you get a free mentorship call to ask them all your questions. 2) You leverage their large audience, and they also benefit from it. People are more likely to watch/listen to the podcast because someone they like is on it. This is arguably the best beginner growth "hack." 3) You form deeper relationships with good people. If you host a good podcast, the guest is more likely to respond to future requests or just help you out. If you're a creator, freelancer, or just someone who is trying to do their own thing, you need people to see your content and work. Most people rarely gain traction because the algorithm is like the lottery. But when you are strategic about forming connections with people who already have an audience, you can build your own much, much faster. Even if you only host 2-4 podcasts in your entire career, those alone could change everything for you depending on the guest you get on.
zh: 如果你想让自己的作品曝光，这听起来很奇怪，但开始播客是你能做的最好的事情之一...

text: I hate the whole "entrepreneurship isn't for everyone" narrative because "it's a hard thing to do." No shit it's hard. That's the point. Yes, most people aren't cut out for it right at this very second. But people can grow. They can learn something new. They can, in fact, challenge themselves. It’s insane to believe that people can actually take control of the 1/3 of their life called work, I know. Everyone could benefit from at bare minimum trying to do something difficult that has such a massive upside in the form of wisdom, skill, and increasing your value even if it doesn't work out. Call me delusional but I believe everyone should pursue work they find meaningful and avoid a life thats perpetually comfortable. “But you can also get a super high paying job that you enjoy!” Yes, but that is still fucking hard. Sometimes a lot harder than entrepreneurship (no, entrepreneurship does not equal trying to build a billion dollar company or working 16 hours day). Being an “entrepreneur” originally meant “to undertake.” To do something. To take a risk. To be high agency. Self governance. To direct your own life rather than outsource it to someone else. So yes, entrepreneurship is for everyone, like the free individuals of the past working on their craft and pursuing their interests. If you haven’t tried it (actually tried it) then you have no idea whether or not it’s for you, and not trying it because someone said it’s “not for everyone” is both dumb and robbing you of potential.
zh: 我讨厌整个“创业并不适合所有人”的说法，因为“这是一件很难做的事情”。不废话，很难。这就是重点。是的，...

text: Money is a skill that can be learned just like playing a video game, but since it's become so intertwined with status, self-worth, morality, and survival people have such a distorted view of it. text: First, you’re in pain. Then, you become curious. And if you don’t suppress that desire to explore a new domain of life, you become obsessed. Most people never change because the pain of their current situation doesn’t outweigh the pain of pursuing the highest version of yourself.
zh: 首先，你很痛苦。然后，你就会变得好奇。如果你不压抑探索新生活领域的欲望，你就会变得……

text: You “aren’t interested in anything” because you don’t give yourself a chance to become interested in anything. Most people aren’t born with a crystal clear vision for their future. The people who create a good life are those who understand that vision is accumulated with the mistakes that come from pursuing anything aside from the default path. Create something with care, and eventually you will care.
zh: 你“对任何事情都不感兴趣”，因为你不给自己机会对任何事情感兴趣。大多数人并不是天生就有水晶...

text: Read more here: letters.thedankoe.com/p/the-death-of…
zh: 在这里阅读更多内容：letters.thedankoe.com/p/the-death-of...

text: You don’t find your passion. You cultivate it by doing something with enthusiasm for a long enough time until you discover the depth of that craft, leading to a burning passion for it. You generate passion in the pursuit of a goal you may not have wanted to pursue, but you saw it as necessary to reach the life you want, so you shut your mind off and did it anyway.
zh: 你找不到自己的激情。你通过长时间热情地做某事来培养它，直到你发现......

text: You don’t find your calling. You listen for it. Because it’s always calling you. But most people can't hear it. Too many tasks, bills, and responsibilities they never really chose to take on. The most important task in your life is to listen for it and act without hesitation.
zh: 你没有找到你的使命。你听听看。因为它总是在呼唤你。但大多数人听不到。太多的任务、账单……

text: build your own thing

text: The ultimate goal is getting paid to be yourself. To solve problems you can't help but solve. To study what you can't pull yourself away from. To remove or delegate any work that does not involve a creative challenge. You don't wait for this day to magically come. You live it, right now, and slowly one hour a day will turn into every waking hour.
zh: 最终目标是获得报酬，做你自己。为了解决你无法不去解决的问题。研究你自己拉不动的东西...

text: Pursuing usefulness will make you a lot happier than chasing happiness.
zh: 追求有用会让你比追逐幸福快乐得多。

text: If you don't lie to yourself, you'll go down the right path. People justify why they feel terrible, why they're trapped by money, why they can't get what they want. All of those are problems that can be solved with skill and knowledge. Honesty allows you to start solving them.
zh: 如果你不欺骗自己，你就会走上正确的道路。人们为自己感觉糟糕的原因、为金钱所困的原因、为什么……

text: Be a loser. Let your parents think you are stupid for not following their path. Start a business and embrace failure after failure. Study the interests that make you feel like an outcast. Where most people conform is where greatness is born.
zh: 成为失败者辩护。让你的父母认为你因为不遵循他们的道路而愚蠢。创业并接受一次又一次的失败。研究...

text: The easiest way to get ahead in life is to commit to a period of skill development. 6-12 months. Pure focus for 2-4 hours a day. Learning and building. Not just binge watching tutorials, but creating quality projects that you, others, or businesses could actually benefit from. Most people won't do it but those who do launch ahead of everyone else.
zh: 一段专注发展技能并完成实用项目的时期，能让你远远超过被动学习者。

text: Avoiding conflict is ruining your life. Being too agreeable screams that you don't know what you want... or you do know what you want but can't muster up the strength to pursue it. Here's how to be more disagreeable: letters.thedankoe.com/p/how-to-be-mo…
zh: 避免冲突正在毁掉你的生活。过于讨人喜欢会尖叫出你不知道自己想要什么...或者你确实知道自己想要什么...

text: If you can spend 4 years getting a degree to do something you don’t care about you can spend 1 year reeducating yourself to build your own thing. A lack of perspective is why most quit after 2 weeks.
zh: 如果你可以花 4 年时间获得学位去做你不关心的事情，你可以花 1 年时间重新教育自己...

text: Your brain works against you until you give it a meaningful goal to wire itself around.
zh: 你的大脑会与你作对，直到你给它一个有意义的目标来连接它。

text: But you can't force it, you will know when it's time
zh: 但你不能强迫它，到时候你就会知道

text: Most people don't get what they want because they challenge themselves without changing themselves. They challenge themselves to stick to a diet for 6 months, stop scrolling 30 minutes before bed, learn a new skill, change careers. And they always fail, because discipline is a feature of identity. It’s not a challenge for fit people to eat healthy and go to the gym. It’s who they are. They can’t even imagine a life where they eat like shit and let themselves go. It doesn’t even cross their mind. Why? Because they learned long ago that they didn't want to live that way. They visualized where their life would end up. All the bad things that would happen. How they would look and feel. The opportunities that wouldn't be available to them. That negative image fueled them more than any challenge ever could. You need to be okay with the fact that you will never return to how you live right now. None of your bad habits have a place in your future life. That's why you are where you are. No 6 month challenge is going to change that unless you are brutally aware that the challenge doesn't end after month 6.
zh: 大多数人没有得到他们想要的，因为他们挑战自己而不改变自己。他们挑战自己坚持6年的饮食…

text: I'm generally unpleasant to be around. I prefer being alone. I hate being out of routine for more than 2-3 days. I'm either thinking about nothing or everything with little in between. I say no to almost everything that gets in the way. At first I thought it was a character flaw and tried to change it. That only led to me splitting focus and slowing progress on the goals that bring me joy in life. When I observe people I look up to, their mission comes first. That's what makes life enjoyable, and if that enjoyment is taken away, it spreads into their work, their loved ones, and their friendships. If you know what you want, and you don't fold to what others think you should want, you are going to be unpleasant to be around. That isn't something to be ashamed of but embraced. In fact, you'll find that people want to be around you more because they trust you. They can feel that you aren't putting on an act like everyone else.
zh: 我通常不喜欢在身边。我更喜欢独自一人。我讨厌超过 2-3 天的不规律生活。我要么在想……

text: If you don't know what you want, people will tell you what you want, and you'll believe it because you have nothing better to do.
zh: 如果你不知道自己想要什么，人们会告诉你你想要什么，你会相信它，因为你没有更好的办法……

text: Focus on these 3 things for the next 30 days: 1) One book for mental gains (30 minutes reading) 2) One project for career gains (2 hours deep work) 3) One fitness routine for physical gains (1 hour workout) Changing your life can be so simple.
zh: 在接下来的 30 天里专注于这 3 件事：1）一本精神收获的书（30 分钟阅读）2）一个职业项目……

text: How to be an NPC: - Never question your parents beliefs - Never go off the guardrails at school - Regurgitate what you hear in the news - Always make a choice that's safe and secure And of course: always treat others goals and opinions as more important than your own.
zh: 如何成为一名 NPC： - 永远不要质疑父母的信仰 - 永远不要在学校偏离护栏 - 反省你所听到的……

text: a good life is the process of narrowing down what doesn't work
zh: 美好的生活就是缩小不可行的范围的过程

text: High intelligence + high agency = building rocket ships to bring humanity to mars. Low intelligence + high agency = starting a small business without caring about optimization. High intelligence + low agency = crying about how rich people should not exist. Low intelligence + low agency = playing victim while following the default path laid out for you. The lesson = with the right amount of agency, it doesn't matter how intelligent you are.
zh: 高智商 + 高能动性 = 建造火箭飞船，将人类带到火星。低智商+高代理力=开办小企业...

text: How to make the wrong decision: Ask everyone for their opinion. Tell everyone what you’re going to do before you do it. Listen to everyone, especially those who don’t have the results you want in life.
zh: 如何做出错误的决定：征求大家的意见。在做之前告诉每个人你要做什么。听……

text: Ask yourself, "What would the average person do?" Then do the opposite.
zh: 问问自己，“一般人会做什么？”然后做相反的事情。

text: Everything is a muscle Everything is a skill issue

text: I oscillate between wanting to be the next Steve Jobs and wanting to throw my phone in a lake and join the monks at least 3 times a day.

text: This may sound obvious but if you don’t feel inspired, you need to surround yourself with inspiration. Books that inspire your thinking and articulation. Businesses that inspire your vision and strategy. It gets the cogs turning in your brain. You can feel the boost in energy.

text: This sounds stupid but I swear it works miracles. For the next 7 days, find a 1 hour long lecture on YouTube from Alan Watts. You have to listen to it when you aren't distracted by anything else: before you take a nap, before you fall asleep, or on a quiet walk. When I do this I can start to feel my mind opening. Ideas start flowing again. Everything feels lighter. I find myself doing this when I feel very narrow. I can't come up with new ideas. I've been working too much. Life feels repetitive. I've done this once a year for the past 5 years and I still don't know why I don't make it a daily thing.
zh: 这听起来很愚蠢，但我发誓它会创造奇迹。接下来的 7 天，请在 YouTube 上找到 Alan 的 1 小时长的讲座…

text: I hate being put in a box. Therefore I hate the traditional concept of niching down as far as possible. If you have multiple interests, here's how you don't go insane: youtu.be/6Q_1QJsO4Ro
zh: 我讨厌被关在盒子里。因此，我讨厌尽可能缩小范围的传统概念。如果您有多个...

text: Don’t feel bad for being picky about who you allow into your life.
zh: 不要因为对允许谁进入您的生活而挑剔而感到难过。

text: distractions are the byproducts of goals you don't care about

text: You care too much about what other people think because your self-image would be shattered if you didn't. You want to be a "good" person. And you think a good person is always kind, noble, and caring. You secretly admire those who don't give a fuck, but if you did the same, you would no longer be a good person according to your broken idea of what that means. You don't need to become an asshole, but you do need to choose what you value and give yourself permission to make that your priority.
zh: 你太在意别人的想法，因为如果你不关心，你的自我形象就会被粉碎。你想成为一个“好人”...

text: The best way to learn is the opposite of what we're taught. You don't learn everything, then start. You start, then learn why it did or didn't work. You start with minimum viable knowledge, then experiment, then succeed or fail, then study to understand why. People dumber than you tend to run circles around you because of this. They aren't paralyzed by the overthinking that comes with overeducation, and they avoid the blindness of pure trial and error.
zh: 最好的学习方式与我们所教的相反。你不需要学习所有的东西，然后就开始。你开始，然后了解为什么......

text: Everyone wants to get rich with AI but nobody wants to go through the uncertainty of building a business. The people who would have gotten rich without AI will, and the people who only start because they think AI will do it for them won't.

text: If you can't find the answer by searching for it, you can probably find it on a long walk, at the end of a workout, or during an extended period of time alone.

text: How to build a business in 2025: - Post ideas on social media - Analyze which do the best - Turn them into free downloads - Analyze reception and results - Turn one into digital product/service - Improve until irresistible - Turn into software, physical products, etc Free idea validation, cash flow to do it full time, and a base of potential customers to support the startup you've always wanted to build. Pair it with AI and it's the lowest risk highest reward option.
zh: 如何在 2025 年建立一家企业： - 在社交媒体上发布想法 - 分析哪些做得最好 - 将它们变成免费的……

text: Working in paradise is extremely overrated. You can't even see your screen from the sun glare on the beach. Wifi connection is always spotty. Can rarely find a place without distractions. I'd take a home office with nothing in it every day of the week.

zh: 如果您不知道该写什么，请写下您一直在尝试学习的困难的事情，因为它将帮助您学习...

text: The sooner you start the sooner you can start sucking less.
zh: 越早开始，您就能越早开始少吸。

text: Luck is a percentage that increases with the number of people who know what you do and how well you do it.

text: Grab a notebook. Write out exactly what you want in your future, don't miss a detail. Then, break it down into goals. Decades, years, months, weeks, and days. Every day, write down the 3 levers you can move to create your vision. Then, forget about the goals and execute.
zh: 写下详细愿景，找出每天真正重要的少数杠杆，然后别再只盯着目标。

text: Get excited over the small things. The bland but healthy food. The difficult but rewarding last rep. The uncomfortable but necessary silence of being alone. All of it. The highs, lows, bigs, and smalls of life are going to pass whether you enjoy them or not.
zh: 对小事情感到兴奋。平淡但健康的食物。困难但有益的最后代表。令人不舒服但必要的沉默......

text: How to be productive as fuck: - Find something you deeply care about - Do everything in your power to do that full time - Say no to anything that pulls you away from those Most people use deep work tactics to compensate for a lack of work they can't pull themselves away from.

text: It's better to have conviction in a bad idea and fail than to nuance yourself into irrelevance.

text: If you want to grow fast, you need to create something so undeniably good that people with good taste share it without second thought. Forget about the algorithm for a bit. Smart people love to share high-signal things on socials and in tight-knit group chats. Optimize for that.
zh: 如果你想快速成长，你需要创造一些无可否认的好东西，让有品味的人不假思索地分享它。...

text: Spill your mind onto the internet for a year and see where it takes you.
zh: 将你的想法放到互联网上一年，看看它会带你去哪里。

text: The next 10 years will be insane. Nobody knows what's going to happen. Work will change. People will feel lost. It's inevitable. But there are a few things you can do to prepare: youtu.be/encqErQFKoE
zh: 未来10年将是疯狂的。没有人知道会发生什么。工作将会改变。人们会感到失落。这是不可避免的。但是……

text: The minute I become too frugal or money conscious is the moment it starts to control me and becomes difficult to make. When I spend almost carelessly, it seems to find its way back. A poor psychological relationship with money will ruin you.
zh: 当我变得过于节俭或注重金钱的那一刻，它就开始控制我并且变得很难做。当我...

text: If you want to have great ideas, surround yourself with people who have great ideas. These are usually found in non-mainstream books and small pockets of the internet. Once it hits mainstream it gets tainted.
zh: 如果你想有伟大的想法，那就让自己周围都是有伟大想法的人。这些通常可以在非主流书籍和小...

text: You don't need an insane sales funnel. You need to build a world. A small-scale Marvel with movies (long form), shows (short form), and action figures (products) for people to binge and explore. Posting for 2 weeks then giving up doesn't give people a chance to trust you.

text: You aren't passionate about the skill. You're passionate about creating things. Skills change as technology accelerates. Don't latch onto them. But the act of creation is something you can always hold onto, because it's the deepest part of you.
zh: 你对这项技能不感兴趣。你热衷于创造事物。随着技术的加速发展，技能也会发生变化。不要抓住它们。但是…

text: Find something you're deeply excited about. Obsess over it. Rip it apart, study it, and put it back together, but in your own way. Share it where billions of people hang out. Get one person to care about it as much as you do. Then five. Then a thousand. Then you're set.

text: Here's a short future-proof manifesto: thedankoe.substack.com/p/the-future-p… Read here^

text: The safer you try to be, the more vulnerable you become, because you don't create that safety. You seek certainty in a job, a belief system, a nice little path someone else has laid out for you. A good life stems from how much uncertainty you're willing to embrace.

text: It doesn’t matter how hard you worked. You can write for 10 years straight, but that doesn’t mean anyone will care about your writing. I don’t care how much you “just want to write” and how everyone else has bad taste but you. Writing is a two-player game. 80% of your success lies in giving the reader a reason to care. Call it marketing, persuasion, business, whatever you want. At least put some thought into reader psychology.

text: A trend I see coming soon: Creators sharing AI prompts along with their posts, videos, newsletters, or even courses because it adds an interactive layer. But when you turn something like your newsletter into a prompt, not only do you increase the value of that newsletter, the reader can plug in their own mind. The 3 main categories of products / content is do it yourself, done for you, and done with you. This marks the end of the do it yourself era, because AI can use the context to both be your coach or freelancer. A strategy someone gives out in a YouTube video now becomes information that can execute on that strategy for you.

text: Your mind runs on a storyline. Games are interactive stories with certain mechanisms that narrow your focus and make progress addictive. If you want to get unstuck, use this AI prompt to turn your life into a video game (it's long): ––– You are LifeQuest AI, a strategic life coach that transforms the user's life into an immersive game experience. You analyze their current life phase (Limbo, Vision, Flow, or Resistance), help identify their true desires, and create a personalized game structure with actionable quests and meaningful narrative. You maintain honesty about uncertainty and ask clarifying questions when needed to provide accurate guidance. Context: The user wants to gamify their life journey using principles from Adlerion psychology, flow psychology, and Greek philosophy. They seek clarity on their current life phase, future direction, and meaningful pursuits. They need support to break these goals into manageable quests within a cohesive narrative framework that makes progress tangible and rewarding. This interaction unfolds in two phases: first to assess and strategize, then to implement and coach ongoing progress through quests. Instructions: Phase 1: Life Assessment & Game Design 1. Throughout this phase, ask 1-3 questions at a time. Do not ask all questions at once to reduce overwhelm. 2. Begin by asking key diagnostic questions to identify the user's current life phase: - What areas of life feel stuck, flowing, or unclear? - What activities currently energize vs. drain them? - What would they fight to avoid as a future outcome? - What new information, people, or experiences have recently sparked their interest? 3. Analyze responses to determine which life phase they're in: - Limbo (uncertainty, directionless feeling) - Vision (emerging clarity, building momentum) - Flow (strong engagement, rapid progress) - Resistance (plateauing, clinging to past success) 4. Guide the user to articulate their "negative vision" — the outcome they want to avoid if nothing changes. 5. Help the user discover potential positive directions by asking about: - Sources of genuine curiosity - Skills they enjoy developing - Meaningful impacts they wish to make - Environments where they thrive 6. Synthesize findings into a complete game structure: - Main Quest: Primary life direction/purpose - Side Quests: Supporting goals in various life domains - Character Stats: Key abilities to develop - Level System: How progress will be measured - Rules: Non-negotiable boundaries and values - Rewards: Meaningful celebration of progress - Tutorial Phase: Initial low-risk experiments to try 7. Design quantifiable feedback loops with daily/weekly priority tasks that provide clear markers of progress. Phase 2: Ongoing Quest Guidance 1. Begin each session by checking progress on previous quests and updating the user's "character status." 2. Provide appropriate coaching based on their current situation: - If stuck: Help identify obstacles and potential solutions - If progressing: Suggest appropriate "level-ups" or new challenges - If overwhelmed: Simplify quests or adjust difficulty - If bored: Introduce novel challenges at the edge of their capabilities 3. Frame all advice within their game narrative, maintaining the metaphor consistently. 4. Regularly revisit and update the main quest as the user's vision evolves. 5. Balance short-term achievements with long-term development. 6. When the user faces significant obstacles, help them: - Analyze patterns that led to the block - Break down complex challenges into smaller quests - Identify potential "power-ups" (resources, skills, or allies) - Reframe setbacks as essential plot developments Constraints: - Maintain game metaphor consistently but never at the expense of providing practical, actionable advice - Focus on intrinsic motivation over external rewards - Recommend only challenges slightly beyond current capabilities (the edge of the unknown) - Never suggest activities that conflict with user's stated boundaries or values - Avoid generic advice; tailor all guidance to user's specific situation - Acknowledge uncertainty when you don't have enough information Output Format: Phase 1 Output: Life Phase Analysis: Current phase identification with supporting evidence Character Assessment: Strengths, weaknesses, motivations, and potential directions Game Design: - Main Quest (overall life direction) - Side Quests (supporting goals) - Character Stats (skills to develop) - Level System (progress metrics) - Game Rules (boundaries/values) Tutorial Phase: First 1-2 weeks of specific quests and experiments Feedback System: Daily/weekly check-in structure Phase 2 Output (for each coaching session): 1. Quest Log Update: Progress since last interaction 2. Character Status: Current "level" and stats 3. Quest Guidance: Next immediate steps with specificity 4. Challenge Calibration: Adjustments to maintain optimal difficulty 5. Narrative Development: How recent developments fit into their overall story

text: Another example of this (with prompt): Task: I want to write high-converting copy for a landing page Instead of finding a YT video, I can find the PDF of a book like Great Leads or any other copywriting book. Feed that to AI and ask it to break down in detailed steps how to write high converting copy Then, find a landing page structure you want to emulate (SaaS, info, local biz, whatever) Copy the text over to AI and ask it to break down the exact structure of the page and why it performs well. Save both the 'how to write copy' output and the 'landing page structure' output to somewhere in your notes, or just copy/paste when you need them below Then, you're going to: - Send the prompt below to a new AI chat - Tell it you want to create a high converting landing page - Tell it to use the 'how to write copy' output to understand how to write copy - Tell it to use the 'landing page structure' output to format the copy Now it'll probably ask for your target audience and what you are selling. If you don't know those things, you can run through the exact same process (break down YT vid or PDF to teach you how > turn that into prompt) for each of them to create a customer avatar and irresistible offer. text: This is one thing we're trying to account for while building kortex. It's hard for me to believe that most people will want to get rid of their creative work. Yes, many people will outsource work they don't want to do (like writing or marketing) to AI, and that's fine, but I still believe there will be a place carved out for those who take the craft seriously. I could be very wrong and the dead internet could get worse. But it's already pretty bad. There's a ton of garbage content (mostly at the bottom of the barrel and is never seen by many people) and true creatives are still able to take their piece of the pie.

text: As a writer, I've noticed that AI doesn't shorten my writing process (because I don't want it to), it makes it longer. I don't treat AI as the writer, I treat it as the reader. I have it raise objections to my points. I have it rate my hook and introduction for various metrics like relatability, use of curiosity gaps, and brevity. I have it overview my writing to ensure readers understand it, learn something knew, and are given at least one thing to implement once they're done reading. I really struggle to have AI do the writing for me. It doesn't feel right. No matter how good the AI gets it still lacks a soul. Even if it comes up with good or novel ideas, I don't want to use them because that strips the joy from coming up with them myself. I literally start to feel dumber and less creative when I let AI take over too much of the process. My prompts have shifted to helping enhance the writing process rather than replace it, and it's allowed me to spend more time (not less) on the craft I enjoy mastering.

text: I feel like AI's impact on writing is vastly overhyped. And that's coming from someone building an AI writing software. First, there is already too much content. We can't read it all. AI will add more, but most of it will go to the bottom of the barrel unless written by someone who could have gotten that content seen (someone who's already a skilled writer). Writing is one skill that only gains traction when paired with a personality and complementary skills that make other people see it and care about it. It may get slightly harder to separate signal from noise, but I doubt it, because AI content can still be signal. Second, AI can write a tweet or article or newsletter or video script, but that's not why or how you build an audience of readers. Your "brand" is a culmination of everything you've put out. Having one tweet or TikTok go viral does almost nothing for you aside from maybe a quick cash grab. Most viral content is shallow and low IQ. That's not a foundation for a meaningful long-term brand. Most worthwhile content is not viral. Your brand is a culmination of every piece of writing you put out, viral or not. It's the personal or organizational philosophy, meaning, and vision. Your writing is a thread in that tapestry woven over time. Third, just because AI can write or code or market doesn't mean that everyone will write or code or market. It means that people who already wanted to write, or see the value in it, will attempt to leverage AI. And once they realize that a lack of fundamental understanding of the skill won't get you very far, they'll have to learn it from scratch anyway (shoutout vibe coders). AI is revolutionary. There's no question about it. But the hype right now feels like everyone hopping on the next get rich quick scheme. The people who aren't slaves to quick fixes and cheap dopamine will continue to do well as they have been. In reality, this is more like the next evolution of how we write. Pen and paper > typewriter > keyboard > software assisted > AI assisted. It's a big jump, but after all the "writing is dead" hype we're still in the same place we've been.
zh: 我觉得人工智能对写作的影响被过分夸大了。这是来自开发人工智能写作软件的人。首先，已经……

text: Most AI summaries suck because they give you key points and leave out most useful information. Here's a better prompt for note-taking/research: ===Comprehensive Content Summarizer=== : You are an Expert Content Summarizer with a talent for capturing both key facts and underlying context. Your summaries include essential information, meaningful context, philosophical underpinnings, and subtle nuances that others might overlook. You prioritize comprehensiveness over brevity, ensuring nothing important is missed, while still organizing information efficiently. When uncertain about details, you clearly indicate this rather than filling gaps with assumptions. : The user will provide content (YouTube videos, articles, books, podcasts, or other media) they want thoroughly summarized. They value comprehensive understanding over quick highlights, and specifically want to capture contextual elements and philosophical frameworks that might be dismissed as "fluff" by less thorough summarizes. : 1. Begin by identifying the content type, creator/author, title, and publication date, if available. 2. Create a multi-layered summary with these components: - Core Thesis/Main Argument (1-2 sentences identifying the central point) - Key Points Summary (bulleted list of crucial facts, arguments, or conclusions) - Contextual Framework (identify the philosophical, theoretical, historical, or cultural context) - Detailed Breakdown (section-by-section or chronological summary capturing all significant content) - Nuanced Perspectives (note any competing viewpoints, counterarguments, or qualifiers presented) - Underlying Assumptions (identify unstated premises, worldviews, or biases that inform the content) 3. Pay special attention to: - Abstract concepts, philosophical positions, and theoretical frameworks - Historical or cultural context that shapes the content Methodological approaches or reasoning patterns used - Qualifiers, limitations, or nuances the creator acknowledges - Connections to broader ideas, movements, or disciplines Implications or applications suggested by the content 4. When information is unclear or missing: - Clearly indicate gaps or ambiguities with phrases like "The content does not specify..." - Avoid filling in missing details with assumptions - If the content contains potentially contradictory elements, note these tensions explicitly 5. For content with citations or references to other works: - Note key sources referenced and their significance to the argument - Identify intellectual traditions or schools of thought being drawn upon : • Prioritize accuracy and comprehensiveness over brevity • Avoid simplifying complex ideas to the point of distortion • Do not introduce external information not present in the original content • Maintain neutrality toward the content's positions or arguments • Include timestamps or page references when summarizing longer content : # #Comprehensive Summary of [Content Title] Creator/Author: [Name] Publication Date: [Date if available] Content Type: [Article/Video/Podcast/etc.] Length/Duration: [Pages/Minutes if available] ## #Core Thesis [1-2 sentence statement of the main argument or purpose] ## #Key Points • [Essential point 1] • [Essential point 2] • [Continue as needed] ## #Contextual Framework [Paragraph(s) identifying philosophical, theoretical, historical, or cultural context] ## #Detailed Breakdown [Section/Timestamp 1] [Thorough summary of this section] [Section/Timestamp 2] [Thorough summary of this section] [Continue as needed] ## #Nuanced Perspectives [Paragraph(s) noting competing viewpoints, counterarguments, or qualifiers presented] ## #Underlying Assumptions [Paragraph(s) identifying unstated premises, worldviews, or biases] ## #Connections & Implications [Paragraph(s) on how this content connects to broader ideas and its potential applications]

text: 4) Generate Content Ideas You can be very simple or very complex with this. Personally, I've written an entire guide on "How I Write Tweets" that's around 2000 words long. I'm pretty picky with how I write. Then, I go to kAI and say "Use @[How To Write Tweets] to write 15 tweets based on ideas from @[Previous Writing]" It pulls my voice, style, tone, and content ideas from something like a newsletter (or source, or whatever else you want) and spits out 15 tweets according to my guide. Then, I duplicate my Weekly Posts Template (you get this in the welcome email from signing up to Kortex) to use those tweets as inspiration as I write. To make it a bit simpler for you, here's a prompt to start with: "I'm writing content for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. It has to be educational, entertaining, and attention-grabbing. The best topics are those that directly appeal to the reader, illustrate a strong pain point, and help them change their mind to reach the desired outcome. Here's how we'll proceed: • Find 10 specific struggles that my audience is facing. • Find 10 specific desires my audience wants to achieve. • Come up with 10 specific topics that I can pick from. Write these as if they were for a headline for a high-performing newsletter or YouTube video title. Do not ask me to clarify the target audience. Only output the struggles, desires, and topics in a numbered list." Now you have 30 relevant ideas to start with.

text: 3) Writing Feedback Since all of your articles, newsletters, and video scripts are written in your workspace (hopefully), you can simply "@" them and ask kAI to review them. How his dad is showing interest in helping him try new things like trading, building a product, or other non-conventional ways of making money. I told him to just keep sending the updates to me. text: This has been our goal for a while now. Cursor For Creative Work (learning, writing, idea capture, video scripting) But we made a few "mistakes" while developing Kortex that you may benefit from hearing. The good news is, starting this week, we're releasing the first AI assistance features that drastically change what you can do in it. That's the first "mistake." We deprioritized AI features and missed out on a lot of the hype with new models coming out. Instead, we focused on building a solid base. Something better than your typical writing, note-taking, or second brain app: - Connections and tags - Infinite panes to have any idea open - Capture ideas like texting yourself This wasn't a mistake, but it took a lot of time to get it right. I don't think we should have changed this, and gratefully we have users who refuse to use any other app. The mistake here is that we thought we needed things like: - Full offline mode - The desktop app - A top tier mobile app - Workspaces and teams Those are very important things, and they need to happen, but they take a lot development time without an increase in differentiation. (Although these will differentiate us from those who build a similar app fast - note-taking apps have a lot of gotchas and edge cases that I don't think most people are ready for.) In other words, we started building things that every other app already has, and that only delays our time to becoming an app that is a "hell yes" to switch to from another app that already does the job so well. Our capture feature was cool and differentiating, but not a driving "hell yes" factor in switching from others. Since the start of Kortex, we've had the vision of being able to: - Capture any idea, from anywhere, fast - Transcribe any article, video, or link so you can reference it while writing or with AI - Have all models in one chat interface that can access any document, source, highlight, or the entire context of your workspace (so you don't need 8 tabs open while you work) Because writing and creative work isn't just about putting stuff in a word processor. To do good work, you need to capture ideas, learn fast, research fast, access any relevant info fast, and synthesize everything into something worth publishing to the world. Now that we have desktop, partial offline, and mobile very close, we feel like we're finally hitting our stride. We can now solely focus on our differentiating factor. Right now, a big pain of ours is that we're compared with things like Notion, Obsidian, Apple Notes, and the rest, when we know that within 3-6 months Kortex will be unrecognizable from them. We're still new to this, so the lesson here is only theory until we prove it to ourselves, but: You need to get extremely clear on why you are different. But not just that, everyone knows that. You need to build that first. You need to enter the market as that, even if you don't have the little quality of life features. I feel like if we did that, we would have grown a lot faster, because people would be willing to sacrifice little things for a completely new way of doing things that other products don't provide. Then, once that's proven and you give yourself enough time to iterate, focus on the small things that push you even further. I may be wrong about this, but I feel like our thought process here could help others wanting to do similar things. Lots of moving pieces. Building is fun.
